title: Speocera vangvieng Jäger & Nophaseud 2026, spec. nov.
author: Jäger, Peter
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-zero
description: Speocera vangvieng  spec. nov.   Figs 14–33, 41–47   Type material.   LAOS:  Vientiane Province : Holotype male,  W Vang Vieng, Tham Phoukham, N 18°55’38.47”, E 102°23’42.20”, 314 m elevation, in cave, 50–300 m from entrance, by hand, by day, P. Jäger leg. 11 October 2022 (SMF).  Paratypes: 1 female , with same data as for holotype (SMF).  1 female,  with same data as for holotype, but P. Jäger leg. 15 November 2025 (LKCNHM).   Additional material examined. LAOS:  Vientiane Province : 1 subadult male, with same data as for holotype (SMF) .  1 male,  West of Vang Vieng, Tham Khan, 18°55’30.66”N, 102°26’3.58”E, 230 m, along cliff, in front of cave, pitfall trap, P. Jäger leg. 4–24 January 2025 (NUOL).  1 male,  West of Vang Vieng, Houay Leng, 18°56’24.49”N, 102°24’14.64”E, 416 m, secondary forest, leaf litter beside empty stream bed, sieving, Winkler, P. Jäger leg. 12 October 2024 (SMF).  5 females, 4 juveniles , North of Vang Vieng, Ban Phoudindeng, Tham Kihit, 18°58’49.65”N, 102°25’36.03”E, 352 m, in cave (entrance to 100 m), by hand, by day, P. Jäger & A. Hudspeth leg. 9 January 2025 (SMF; 1 female: NUOL).   Etymology.  The species name is derived from the type locality; name in apposition.   Diagnosis.  Males of  Speocera vangvieng  spec. nov. can be distinguished from all other congeners by the presence of two stout cymbial spines located subdistally on the retrolateral side (Fig. 15). Males can also be diagnosed by the ratio in their palpal segments, i.e. tibia length/tibia width: 2.44 and tibia length/tarsus length: 1.40 (Fig. 15; tibia length/tibia width: 1.52–2.32 in 19 species [cf. Tong  et al . 2019] with the following exceptions:  S. bukittinggi  Li & Li, in Tong  et al ., 2019 [2.57] and  S. suea  Li & Li, in Tong  et al ., 2019 [2.56]; tibia length/tarsus length: 0.74–1.28 in 19 species [cf. Tong  et al . 2019] with the following exceptions:  S. ankalana  Li & Li, in Tong  et al ., 2019 [1.4],  S. bukittinggi  Li & Li, in Tong  et al ., 2019 [1.88] and  S. payakumbuh  Li & Li, in Tong  et al ., 2019 [1.88]). Females are most similar to those of  S. soksay  spec. nov. in having similarly shaped afferent and median spermathecae (Figs 18, 20–21, 23–30), but can be distinguished by having smaller eyes and apical end of afferent spermathecae barely bent (eyes larger in  S. soksay  spec. nov.).   Description. Male  (holotype). PL 0.44, PW 0.37, AW 0.21, OL 0.43, OW 0.29. Eye diameters: PME 0.01, ALE 0.01, PLE 0.01. Palp measurements: 0.73 (0.28, 0.09, 0.22, -, 0.14 [bulb 0.16]), all legs missing. Chelicerae with 1 small retromarginal tooth, and 4/6 promarginal teeth and an unidentate lamina (Fig. 17).  Palp as in diagnosis (Figs 14–15). Bulb oval, embolus slightly elongated and with a short tooth-like projection.  Colouration (Figs 41–43). Pale yellowish without pattern, eye region somewhat darker.   Female  (paratype). PL 0.50, PW 0.40, AW 0.26, OL 0.62, OW 0.34. Eye diameters: PME 0.015, ALE 0.015, PLE 0.015. Palp and leg measurements: 0.46 (0.15, 0.07, 0.13, -, 0.11), I–II, IV missing, III 1.53 (0.46, 0.14, 0.42, 0.35, 0.26).  Copulatory organ as in diagnosis (Figs 18, 23–30). Afferent spermathecae strongly elongated, reaching into dorsal half of opisthosoma and being distinctly longer that anterior width of prosoma, only very slightly curved in their dorsal part. Median spermathecae elongate oval, bent at their basal stalks.  Colouration (Figs 44–46). As in male.   Variation.  Male PL 0.50, PW 0.40–0.42, AW 0.22–0.24, OL 0.46–0.55, OW 0.31–0.35. Eye diameters: PME 0.03,ALE 0.03, PLE 0.03. Eyes with pigments, dorsal prosoma with striae and marginal marbled pattern; opisthosoma with faint grey colouration. Female PL 0.52–0.54, PW 0.42, AW 0.25, OL 0.57–0.66, OW 0.47. Eye diameters: PME 0.022, ALE 0.027, PLE 0.023. Palp and leg measurements: palp 0.49 (0.17, 0.07, 0.13, -, 0.12), I 1.98 (0.59, 0.15, 0.60, 0.38, 0.26), II 1.865 (0.55, 0.155, 0.55, 0.26, 0.25). Eyes region with black pigments (Fig. 31).   Distribution.  Known from four localities in the Vangvieng region, Vientiane Province, Laos (Fig. 47).   Biology.  There are each two records of subterranean and epigean populations. In all specimens recorded, six eyes were present, in all populations but the type locality, pigments were present in eyes and as body colouration. No differences in the genitalia could be recognised. It is assumed that there are subterranean and surface populations of the same, relatively widespread species like, for instance, in  Porrhomma egeria  Simon, 1884 or  Bathyphantes improbulus  (Simon, 1929) (Růžička 1999). One female was observed in its curled sheet web in small holes of a flow stone (Fig. 45) to carry one egg in its fangs (Fig. 46). Another female was recorded holding two hatched spiderlings in its fangs (Fig. 44). These spiderlings had almost half of the size of their mother.
